content.text:
Good direction — a separate worker container keeps the panel slim and matches how fetch already works. I'll draft a concrete plan around that.

input.name: Scheduled fetch worker
input.overview: Add application-level 6-hour job fetch scheduling as a dedicated Playwright worker container on EC2, reusing the existing fetch pipeline while keeping the panel container slim and read-only.
input.plan:
# Scheduled company job fetch (6h, EC2 worker)

## Opinion

This is the right direction. Manual country fetch is easy to forget; a 6-hour cadence keeps `matching_jobs` fresh without you clicking Fetch. Putting the **schedule logic in Python** (`relocation_jobs/fetch/scheduler.py`) is cleaner than host crontab one-liners — deploy still starts a container, but *when* and *what* to fetch lives in the app.

Your choice of a **separate worker container** is the best fit for this repo:

- Panel stays on [`Dockerfile.ec2`](Dockerfile.ec2) (no Playwright, fast restarts, low RAM).
- Fetch reuses the proven path: `start_country_fetch` → [`fetch/country_runner.py`](relocation_jobs/fetch/country_runner.py) → `sync_company_board_to_catalog`.
- Cross-process safety already exists: [`fetch_is_running()`](relocation_jobs/fetch/runner.py) checks Postgres `fetch_runs.status = 'running'`, so a local manual fetch and the EC2 worker won't stomp each other (409 / skip).

**Caveats to accept upfront:**

| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|------------|
| `t4g.micro` (~1 GB RAM) running Postgres + Redis + panel + worker | Worker concurrency **4** (not 16); countries run **sequentially** per cycle |
| Playwright image ~400 MB+ | Separate image; panel image unchanged |
| Long fetch blocks next scheduled tick | Scheduler waits for thread join; if still running at next tick, skip |
| Race on simultaneous `create_fetch_run` | Rare; optional follow-up: Postgres advisory lock in `start_country_fetch` |

Panel on EC2 stays `PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=0` — users see fetch history/status; scrape runs only in the worker.

---

## Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph ec2 [EC2 host]
    Panel[relocation-panel\nslim image\nscrape off]
    Worker[relocation-fetch-worker\nPlaywright image\nscheduler loop]
    PG[(Postgres)]
    Redis[(Redis)]
  end
  Panel --> PG
  Panel --> Redis
  Worker --> PG
  Worker -->|"every 6h: country fetch"| PG
```

**One cycle (all countries, sequential):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  participant S as scheduler
  participant R as fetch/runner
  participant DB as Postgres
  S->>DB: fetch_is_running?
  alt busy
    S->>S: skip tick
  else idle
    loop each country
      S->>R: start_country_fetch(admin_user, country)
      R->>DB: INSERT fetch_runs
      R->>R: scrape ATS boards
      R->>DB: sync matching_jobs
      S->>R: wait thread join
    end
  end
```

---

## Implementation

### 1. Scheduler module — [`relocation_jobs/fetch/scheduler.py`](relocation_jobs/fetch/scheduler.py) (new)

Core application logic, env-driven:

| Env var | Default | Purpose |
|---------|---------|---------|
| `FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED` | `0` | Worker sets `1`; panel stays `0` |
| `FETCH_SCHEDULE_INTERVAL_HOURS` | `6` | Sleep between cycles |
| `FETCH_SCHEDULE_CONCURRENCY` | `4` | Passed to `start_country_fetch` (cap at `MAX_CONCURRENCY`) |
| `FETCH_SCHEDULE_COUNTRIES` | all `supported_countries()` | Comma-separated override |

Functions:

- `schedule_enabled()` — read env
- `resolve_scheduler_user_id()` — lookup `PANEL_ADMIN_USER` in [`db/users.py`](relocation_jobs/db/users.py) (small helper)
- `run_fetch_cycle()` — if `fetch_is_running()`, log and return; else iterate countries, call `start_country_fetch(...)`, join `_fetch_thread` between countries
- `run_scheduler_loop()` — init DB + `configure_fetch_logging()` + `reap_orphan_running_fetch_runs()`, then sleep/loop

No Flask dependency; callable from a standalone script.

### 2. Worker entrypoint — [`scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py`](scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py) (new)

Thin CLI:

```bash
python3 scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py          # long-running loop
python3 scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py --once   # single cycle (debug)
```

### 3. Worker Docker image — [`Dockerfile.ec2-worker`](Dockerfile.ec2-worker) (new)

Based on main [`Dockerfile`](Dockerfile) (Playwright + Chromium), **not** `Dockerfile.ec2`:

```dockerfile
CMD ["python3", "scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py"]
```

Env defaults: `PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=1`, `FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED=1`.

### 4. Deploy — extend [`scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh`](scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh)

After panel start, add worker block:

- Build `relocation-fetch-worker:ec2` from `Dockerfile.ec2-worker`
- Run `relocation-fetch-worker` container (no published ports, `--restart unless-stopped`)
- Same `DATABASE_URL` as panel; **no** `REDIS_URL` needed for fetch
- `cmd_status` — include worker container + `docker logs relocation-fetch-worker --tail 20`

Optional subcommand `worker-logs` for ops.

### 5. Local dev

Document running the worker alongside the panel:

```bash
FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED=1 PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=1 python3 scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py
```

Local panel can keep manual Fetch; scheduler respects `fetch_is_running()`.

### 6. Docs

Update [`docs/operations/ec2-panel.md`](docs/operations/ec2-panel.md):

- Stack table adds `relocation-fetch-worker`
- Note RAM/concurrency expectations
- Log inspection commands

Brief mention in [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) commands section.

### 7. Tests — [`tests/fetch/test_scheduler.py`](tests/fetch/test_scheduler.py) (new)

- `run_fetch_cycle` skips when DB has a running fetch run
- `--once` triggers `start_country_fetch` for configured countries (monkeypatch runner, no Playwright)

---

## What we are **not** doing (scope guard)

- No scheduler thread inside gunicorn (fragile with worker restarts and memory spikes)
- No change to `build_companies.py` / careers URL discovery (different pipeline, Playwright-heavy, lower cadence if needed later)
- No host crontab (container loop is sufficient; logic stays in app code)
- No panel scrape re-enable on EC2

---

## Rollout

1. Implement scheduler + worker script + tests locally with `--once`
2. Add `Dockerfile.ec2-worker` + deploy changes
3. `./scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh deploy` — verify `docker ps` shows worker
4. Watch `docker logs relocation-fetch-worker` through first cycle; confirm `fetch_runs` rows and new jobs in board

If OOM on micro instance: lower `FETCH_SCHEDULE_CONCURRENCY` to 2 or upsize to `t4g.small`.

Add scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py (--once flag) and resolve_scheduler_user_id helper
Extend scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh to build/run relocation-fetch-worker container
Add tests/fetch/test_scheduler.py for skip-when-busy and cycle trigger
Update docs/operations/ec2-panel.md and CLAUDE.md with worker stack and ops commands

input.contents:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Long-running fetch scheduler worker (EC2 or local)."""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import sys
from pathlib import Path

ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1]
if str(ROOT) not in sys.path:
    sys.path.insert(0, str(ROOT))


def main() -> int:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Run scheduled country job fetches.")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--once",
        action="store_true",
        help="Run a single fetch cycle and exit.",
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    from relocation_jobs.fetch.scheduler import (
        bootstrap_scheduler,
        run_fetch_cycle,
        run_scheduler_loop,
        schedule_enabled,
    )

    if args.once:
        bootstrap_scheduler()
        if not schedule_enabled():
            print("FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED is off", file=sys.stderr)
            return 1
        result = run_fetch_cycle()
        print(result)
        return 0 if not result.get("skipped") or result.get("reason") == "fetch_busy" else 0

    run_scheduler_loop()
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

# EC2 fetch worker (Playwright + scheduled country fetches).
FROM python:3.12-bookworm

ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PANEL_DATA_DIR=/tmp/panel-data \
    PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=1 \
    FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED=1 \
    FETCH_SCHEDULE_INTERVAL_HOURS=6 \
    FETCH_SCHEDULE_CONCURRENCY=4

WORKDIR /app

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    ca-certificates \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt \
    && playwright install --with-deps chromium

COPY . .
RUN chmod +x scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py

RUN mkdir -p /tmp/panel-data

CMD ["python3", "scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py"]

${PANEL_IMAGE}
EOF

  log "Building ${WORKER_IMAGE} on EC2 (Playwright, may take several minutes)..."
  ssh_cmd "cd ${REMOTE_DIR} && docker build -f Dockerfile.ec2-worker -t ${WORKER_IMAGE} ."

  log "Starting fetch worker container..."
  ssh_cmd bash -s <<EOF
set -euo pipefail
docker rm -f ${WORKER_CONTAINER} 2>/dev/null || true
docker run -d --name ${WORKER_CONTAINER} --restart unless-stopped \\
  -e PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=1 \\
  -e FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED=1 \\
  -e FETCH_SCHEDULE_INTERVAL_HOURS=6 \\
  -e FETCH_SCHEDULE_CONCURRENCY=4 \\
  -e PANEL_ADMIN_USER=admin \\
  -e PANEL_ADMIN_PASSWORD='${admin_pass}' \\
  -e DATABASE_URL='${db_url}' \\
  ${WORKER_IMAGE}
EOF

  log "Starting Caddy reverse proxy..."

cmd_status() {
  load_state
  ssh_cmd "docker ps --filter name=relocation- --format 'table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}'"
  log "Fetch worker logs (last 20 lines):"
  ssh_cmd "docker logs ${WORKER_CONTAINER} --tail 20 2>&1" || log "  worker not running"
  log "Health (via Elastic IP):"
  curl -sf -o /dev/null -w "  http://${ELASTIC_IP}/api/auth/status -> %{http_code}\n" \
    --max-time 30 "http://${ELASTIC_IP}/api/auth/status" || log "  health check failed (DNS/Caddy still starting?)"
}

cmd_worker_logs() {
  load_state
  local tail_n="${2:-100}"
  ssh_cmd "docker logs ${WORKER_CONTAINER} --tail ${tail_n} -f 2>&1"
}

input.contents:
from __future__ import annotations

import pytest

from relocation_jobs.fetch import repo as fetch_repo
from relocation_jobs.fetch.scheduler import (
    run_fetch_cycle,
    schedule_concurrency,
    schedule_countries,
    schedule_enabled,
)


def test_schedule_enabled_reads_env(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.delenv("FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED", raising=False)
    assert schedule_enabled() is False
    monkeypatch.setenv("FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED", "1")
    assert schedule_enabled() is True


def test_schedule_countries_defaults_to_supported(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.delenv("FETCH_SCHEDULE_COUNTRIES", raising=False)
    countries = schedule_countries()
    assert "uk" in countries
    assert "netherlands" in countries


def test_schedule_countries_override(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv("FETCH_SCHEDULE_COUNTRIES", "uk,netherlands")
    assert schedule_countries() == ("uk", "netherlands")


def test_schedule_concurrency_caps_at_max(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv("FETCH_SCHEDULE_CONCURRENCY", "99")
    from relocation_jobs.core.ats_constants import MAX_CONCURRENCY

    assert schedule_concurrency() == MAX_CONCURRENCY


def test_run_fetch_cycle_skips_when_busy(db, monkeypatch):
    from relocation_jobs.db.users import resolve_scheduler_user_id

    del db
    monkeypatch.setenv("FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED", "1")
    user_id = resolve_scheduler_user_id()
    fetch_repo.create_fetch_run(
        user_id=user_id,
        country="uk",
        company_name=None,
        file_name="uk_companies.json",
        concurrency=1,
        started_at="2025-06-01T12:00:00+00:00",
    )

    result = run_fetch_cycle(user_id=user_id)

    assert result["skipped"] is True
    assert result["reason"] == "fetch_busy"


def test_run_fetch_cycle_starts_configured_countries(db, monkeypatch):
    from relocation_jobs.db.users import resolve_scheduler_user_id

    del db
    monkeypatch.setenv("FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED", "1")
    monkeypatch.setenv("FETCH_SCHEDULE_COUNTRIES", "uk,netherlands")
    monkeypatch.setenv("FETCH_SCHEDULE_CONCURRENCY", "2")

    started: list[tuple[int, str, int]] = []

    def fake_start_country_fetch(**kwargs):
        started.append((kwargs["user_id"], kwargs["country_key"], kwargs["concurrency"]))
        return len(started)

    monkeypatch.setattr(
        "relocation_jobs.fetch.scheduler.start_country_fetch",
        fake_start_country_fetch,
    )
    monkeypatch.setattr(
        "relocation_jobs.fetch.scheduler.wait_for_fetch_thread",
        lambda timeout=None: True,
    )

    user_id = resolve_scheduler_user_id()
    result = run_fetch_cycle(user_id=user_id)

    assert result["skipped"] is False
    assert result["started"] == ["uk", "netherlands"]
    assert started == [
        (user_id, "uk", 2),
        (user_id, "netherlands", 2),
    ]


def test_run_fetch_cycle_disabled(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv("FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED", "0")
    result = run_fetch_cycle(user_id=1)
    assert result["skipped"] is True
    assert result["reason"] == "schedule_disabled"


def test_resolve_scheduler_user_id(db):
    from relocation_jobs.db.users import resolve_scheduler_user_id

    del db
    user_id = resolve_scheduler_user_id()
    assert user_id > 0


def test_resolve_scheduler_user_id_missing_user(monkeypatch):
    from relocation_jobs.db.users import resolve_scheduler_user_id

    monkeypatch.setenv("PANEL_ADMIN_USER", "no-such-admin")
    with pytest.raises(LookupError, match="no-such-admin"):
        resolve_scheduler_user_id()

input.new_string:
if args.once:

        if not schedule_enabled():
            print("FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED is off", file=sys.stderr)
            return 1
        result = run_fetch_cycle()
        print(result)
        return 0

        print(result)

input.new_string:
| Service | Container | Port |
|---------|-----------|------|
| Postgres | `pg` | 5432 |
| Redis | `relocation-redis` | 6379 |
| Panel (gunicorn) | `relocation-panel` | 127.0.0.1:10000 |
| Fetch worker (scheduler) | `relocation-fetch-worker` | — |
| Caddy (TLS + reverse proxy) | `relocation-caddy` | 80, 443 |

Panel talks to Postgres/Redis via Docker bridge gateway `172.17.0.1` (localhost on the host). The fetch worker only needs Postgres; it runs country scrapes every **6 hours** (sequential countries, concurrency **4**).

---

## Deploy / update

From repo root (SSH key `~/Downloads/relocation.pem`, `aws-postgres.env` present):

```bash
./scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh deploy        # rsync + build panel + worker + Caddy
./scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh status        # containers + health check + worker logs
./scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh worker-logs   # follow fetch scheduler logs
```

`deploy` also opens security group ports **80** and **443**.

| Image | Dockerfile | Role |
|-------|------------|------|
| `relocation-panel:ec2` | `Dockerfile.ec2` | Slim panel — no Playwright; `PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=0` |
| `relocation-fetch-worker:ec2` | `Dockerfile.ec2-worker` | Playwright + 6h scheduler; writes to shared Postgres |

Manual scrape from your laptop still works (`PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=1`); the worker skips a cycle if another fetch is already running (`fetch_runs.status = running`).

**Worker env (set by deploy):** `FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED=1`, `FETCH_SCHEDULE_INTERVAL_HOURS=6`, `FETCH_SCHEDULE_CONCURRENCY=4`. Optional override: `FETCH_SCHEDULE_COUNTRIES=uk,netherlands`.

On `t4g.micro`, if the worker OOMs during fetch, lower concurrency to `2` or upsize the instance.

content.text:
Implemented the scheduled fetch worker per the plan. Summary:

### Application logic
- **[`relocation_jobs/fetch/scheduler.py`](relocation_jobs/fetch/scheduler.py)** — env-driven 6h scheduler: `run_fetch_cycle()` runs countries sequentially, skips when busy/disabled, uses concurrency 4 by default
- **[`relocation_jobs/fetch/runner.py`](relocation_jobs/fetch/runner.py)** — added `wait_for_fetch_thread()` for the scheduler to block between countries
- **[`relocation_jobs/db/users.py`](relocation_jobs/db/users.py)** — added `resolve_scheduler_user_id()` (looks up `PANEL_ADMIN_USER`)

### Worker entrypoint
- **[`scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py`](scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py)** — long-running loop or `--once` for debugging

### EC2 deployment
- **[`Dockerfile.ec2-worker`](Dockerfile.ec2-worker)** — Playwright image with scheduler as CMD
- **[`scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh`](scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh)** — builds/starts `relocation-fetch-worker`, adds `worker-logs` subcommand, status shows worker logs

### Tests & docs
- **[`tests/fetch/test_scheduler.py`](tests/fetch/test_scheduler.py)** — 9 tests, all passing
- Updated [`docs/operations/ec2-panel.md`](docs/operations/ec2-panel.md) and [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md)

### Deploy
```bash
./scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh deploy
./scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh worker-logs
```

Local one-shot:
```bash
FETCH_SCHEDULE_ENABLED=1 PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=1 python3 scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py --once
```

Panel stays slim (`PANEL_SCRAPE_ENABLED=0`); scraping runs only in the worker container. If the micro instance OOMs, set `FETCH_SCHEDULE_CONCURRENCY=2` on the worker.

content.text:
The build failed because [`.dockerignore`](.dockerignore) excludes the whole `scripts/` directory, so `scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py` never made it into the Docker context on EC2. The `chmod` step then failed on a file that wasn't there.

Two changes:

1. **`.dockerignore`** — allow the worker script through:
   ```
   scripts/
   !scripts/fetch_scheduler_worker.py
   ```

2. **`Dockerfile.ec2-worker`** — removed the unnecessary `chmod` (the CMD already runs it with `python3`).

Redeploy:

```bash
./scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh deploy
```

[REDACTED]

Deploy succeeded — all containers are up and the worker is fetching. The health check hits the Elastic IP, which Caddy intentionally returns 404 for. Updating the status check to probe the panel directly and treat IP 404 as expected.

[REDACTED]

input.new_string:
cmd_status() {
  load_state
  local domain="${PANEL_DOMAIN:-kuchup.com}"
  local panel_code domain_code ip_code

  ssh_cmd "docker ps --filter name=relocation- --format 'table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}'"
  log "Fetch worker logs (last 20 lines):"
  ssh_cmd "docker logs ${WORKER_CONTAINER} --tail 20 2>&1" || log "  worker not running"

  log "Panel health (localhost:${PANEL_PORT} on EC2):"
  panel_code="$(ssh_cmd "curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 10 http://127.0.0.1:${PANEL_PORT}/api/auth/status" 2>/dev/null || echo 000)"
  if [[ "$panel_code" == "200" ]]; then
    log "  http://127.0.0.1:${PANEL_PORT}/api/auth/status -> ${panel_code} (OK)"
  else
    log "  http://127.0.0.1:${PANEL_PORT}/api/auth/status -> ${panel_code} (FAILED)"
  fi

  log "Panel health (via domain):"
  domain_code="$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 30 "https://${domain}/api/auth/status" 2>/dev/null || echo 000)"
  if [[ "$domain_code" == "200" ]]; then
    log "  https://${domain}/api/auth/status -> ${domain_code} (OK)"
  else
    log "  https://${domain}/api/auth/status -> ${domain_code} (check DNS/TLS if deploy just finished)"
  fi

  log "Origin lock-down (Elastic IP, expect 404):"
  ip_code="$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 10 "http://${ELASTIC_IP}/api/auth/status" 2>/dev/null || echo 000)"
  log "  http://${ELASTIC_IP}/api/auth/status -> ${ip_code}"
}
input.old_string:
cmd_status() {
  load_state
  ssh_cmd "docker ps --filter name=relocation- --format 'table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}'"
  log "Fetch worker logs (last 20 lines):"
  ssh_cmd "docker logs ${WORKER_CONTAINER} --tail 20 2>&1" || log "  worker not running"
  log "Health (via Elastic IP):"
  curl -sf -o /dev/null -w "  http://${ELASTIC_IP}/api/auth/status -> %{http_code}\n" \
    --max-time 30 "http://${ELASTIC_IP}/api/auth/status" || log "  health check failed (DNS/Caddy still starting?)"
}

content.text:
This is not a deploy failure — it's a misleading health check.

Your [`Caddyfile`](deploy/ec2/Caddyfile) deliberately returns **404** on the Elastic IP (`http://3.76.111.159`). Only `kuchup.com` / `www` proxy to the panel. The old status script treated that 404 as failure.

From your terminal output, deploy actually **succeeded**:
- All 4 containers are up (panel, worker, Caddy, Redis)
- Fetch worker started and is already scraping Armenia

Verify the panel is healthy:

```bash
curl -sf https://kuchup.com/api/auth/status
./scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh status   # after pulling the script fix
```

I updated [`scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh`](scripts/ec2_app_deploy.sh) `cmd_status` to:
1. Check panel on `localhost:10000` via SSH (real health)
2. Check `https://kuchup.com/api/auth/status` (public path)
3. Show Elastic IP 404 as **expected** origin lock-down, not an error

[REDACTED]
